method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
The Hunga volcano is situated on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The arc is part of a subduction system extending about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[4] The volcano is largely submarine, rising more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extending about 20 km across.[4] Its summit is occupied by a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter.[4]

Before the 2022 eruption, the only emergent parts of the volcanic system were the small uninhabited islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, interpreted as remnants of the caldera rim.[2][4] Bryan et al. described the two islands as elongated and tangent to a circle centred on a rocky shoal south of Hunga Tonga, the site of eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[2] Rock samples from Hunga Tonga showed alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli, and ash dipping gently away from the inferred centre of the volcanic structure.[2] On this basis, the islands were hypothesised to be the northern and western remnants of a ridge around a large submarine caldera atop an active volcano.[2] Later high-resolution multibeam sonar surveys identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the inferred caldera location, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2]

Hunga’s eruptive products are andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic arc volcanoes.[4] The 2014–2015 eruption built a new tephra cone that connected Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, forming the single island known as Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4][5] That island covered just over 1 km² and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m before the 2022 eruption.[5] The 15 January 2022 eruption was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may rank as high as VEI 5, although the erupted volume required further analysis.[5] The eruption destroyed the joined island, leaving only small remnants of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above sea level.[4][5]

==Geography==
The site lies in the South Pacific within the Tonga archipelago, about 30 km south of Fonuafoʻou and 65 km north of Tongatapu, Tonga’s main island.[4] It occupies a position between the Haʻapai island group and Tongatapu, with ʻEua also lying within the wider area affected by ashfall from the 2022 eruption.[4] Geologically, the volcano is part of the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5]

Before its destruction in 2022, the subaerial island provided a rare example of a newly formed volcanic landform that could be monitored by satellites from its emergence through rapid erosion and geomorphic change.[5] NASA Earth Observatory noted that observations of its evolving shape were useful not only for understanding coastal and volcanic erosion on Earth, but also for interpreting similar landforms observed on Mars.[5] After the January 2022 eruption, the formerly continuous landmass was reduced to small remnants at the western and eastern edges of the former island.[4] Ash from the eruption was deposited across the Haʻapai, Tongatapu, and ʻEua island groups, with reported thicknesses ranging from about 5 to 50 mm and around 20–30 mm on Tongatapu.[4]

==Eruptions==
The paroxysmal 2022 eruption began violently on 15 January at about 5 p.m. local time, producing massive explosions and sending gas and ash high into the atmosphere.[4] Satellite-era observations showed a plume more than 30 km tall, with overshooting tops above 55 km, while another account described material reaching about 36 miles into the atmosphere.[5][4] Estimates from surface-pressure data put the energy release of the initial explosion at 10–28 EJ, probably greater than that of the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption and possibly comparable to the 1883 Krakatoa eruption.[5]

The eruption was interpreted as an unusually intense submarine explosive event, driven by interaction between very hot magma and seawater.[3] NASA scientist James Garvin described it as “not your standard Surtseyan eruption” and said some volcanologists informally called it an “ultra Surtseyan” eruption because of the unusually large amount of water involved.[3] One proposed mechanism was that weakening and partial collapse of the caldera’s northern rim allowed large volumes of seawater to enter a hot underground magma chamber, greatly increasing explosivity.[3] The lack of seismometers on the volcano limited direct reconstruction of the pre-eruptive processes.[3]

The eruption generated tsunamis that struck Tonga, where run-up reached as high as 65 ft above sea level, destroying hundreds of structures, displacing more than 1,500 people, and causing four deaths.[4] Tsunami waves also crossed the Pacific, causing two deaths and an oil spill in Peru and producing damage or impacts in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4] Post-eruption seafloor surveys found fine sandy mud and deep ash ripples as far as 50 km from the volcano, together with gouged valleys and large sediment piles.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable was severed and later found covered by about 100 ft of sediment and ash.[4]

Because the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 miles away, automated earthquake detection around the volcano was difficult during and after the eruption.[4] The USGS National Earthquake Information Center and the USGS Volcano Disaster Assistance Program developed methods to detect earthquakes missed by automated systems and produced a near-real-time earthquake catalog for the area after the eruption.[4]

The eruption injected large quantities of volcanic aerosols and water vapor into the atmosphere, including an unprecedented amount of water vapor for a submarine eruption observed by modern satellites.[2] The stratospheric water content increased by about 10%, creating a climate-forcing situation different from large subaerial eruptions such as Tambora in 1815 and Pinatubo in 1991.[2] The 15 January eruption occurred from a vent about 200 m below the ocean surface and emitted water vapor along with a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide, which rapidly formed sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight.[1] Subsequent studies found that the eruption did not cause the expected global warming effect and instead produced a slight net cooling from 2022, including about 0.1 °C of cooling over the Southern Hemisphere.[1] Researchers attributed the Southern Hemisphere cooling mainly to sunlight-reflecting particles, while noting that lingering stratospheric water vapor may have produced a very small warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere.[1]

==Footnotes==
The 15 January 2022 eruption occurred at the submarine Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai volcano, about 150 m below sea level in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc.[1] It has been described as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern geophysical instrumentation, with an estimated VEI of about 6 and a plume reaching up to 57 km in altitude.[1] The eruption dispersed tephra across an area at least 600 km in diameter and released about 2.9 × 10^15 g of basaltic-andesitic volcanogenic material into the South Pacific Gyre.[1] Contemporary reports described it as the largest recorded eruption in more than three decades and noted tsunami waves affecting Tonga as well as distant coasts in Australia, Japan, and the Americas.[2] One proposed trigger for the eruption was a submarine landslide, which may have removed overlying rock and allowed a highly explosive release.[2] Researchers have argued that the event has major implications for understanding historical and prehistoric Pacific tsunamis and for studying tsunami deposits around South Pacific stratovolcanoes.[3] Climate studies found that the eruption injected exceptional amounts of water vapor into the stratosphere, producing a prolonged cooling of the mid-stratosphere and warming of the lower stratosphere.[4] A UCLA-led analysis concluded that the eruption produced net cooling in the Southern Hemisphere of about 0.1 °C by late 2022 and 2023, partly because small sulfate aerosols efficiently reflected sunlight despite the large water-vapor injection.[5]
